perturbation.original_text: 1.4 "Business Day" shall mean any day other than Saturdays, Sundays and Dutch generally recognized public holidays.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.4 "Business Day" shall mean any day other than Sundays and public holidays observed by T-Mobile Netherlands B.V., regardless of whether they are Dutch generally recognized public holidays.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding which public holidays are excluded, potentially conflicting with the statutory definition of 'Dutch generally recognized public holidays'. By linking it to T-Mobile's observance, the definition becomes subjective and potentially misaligned.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Burgerlijk Wetboek (Civil Code) - specifically articles related to legal holidays and their effect on contractual obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: Dutch law provides a specific list/definition of public holidays. By stating 'public holidays observed by T-Mobile' the contract creates its own definition that could deviate from the legal definition, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a contradiction by defining 'Business Day' based on holidays observed by T-Mobile Netherlands B.V., potentially deviating from the legally recognized 'Dutch generally recognized public holidays.' This is a Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction. The law explanation supports this, stating that Dutch law provides a specific definition of public holidays, and the contract's definition could deviate from it. Scraped Law Snippet 1 includes the 'Burgerlijk Wetboek Boek 3,' indicating the existence of codified Dutch law, even if the snippet itself doesn't directly list public holidays, it shows that Dutch Law exists. The original text refers to 'Dutch generally recognized public holidays' indicating a legal or statutory definition, which the changed text undermines by creating a company specific definition of what constitutes a holiday.

perturbation.original_text: 12.1 Without prejudice to the provisions expressly stated elsewhere in this Agreement, a Party's liability for damage suffered by the other Party, attributable to the first mentioned Party or a person for whom it is liable by law, shall be limited to the following events, and the following amounts: a) for direct damage to physical goods (property damage or "zaakschade") or directly resulting from death or personal injury: up to a maximum of *** per event or series of connected events and up to a further maximum of *** for all events (connected or not) in any period of 12 calendar months;
perturbation.changed_text: 12.1 Without prejudice to the provisions expressly stated elsewhere in this Agreement, a Party's liability for damage suffered by the other Party, attributable to the first mentioned Party or a person for whom it is liable by law, shall be limited to the following events, and the following amounts: a) for reasonably foreseeable damage to physical goods (tangible property damage) or directly resulting from severe discomfort: up to a maximum of *** per event or series of connected events and up to a further maximum of *** for all events (connected or not) in any period of 12 calendar months;
perturbation.explanation: The terms 'direct damage to physical goods (property damage or zaakschade)' and 'directly resulting from death or personal injury' are replaced by 'reasonably foreseeable damage to physical goods (tangible property damage) or directly resulting from severe discomfort'. This weakens the scope of liability. Dutch law provides specific categories for damages (zaakschade, letselschade). 'Severe discomfort' is not a legally recognized category, and 'reasonably foreseeable' adds a subjective element to property damage, contradicting established liability principles.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Burgerlijk Wetboek (Civil Code) - specifically articles related to liability for damages, including Book 6, Articles 95-110 regarding the types and extent of recoverable damages.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 96 and related articles in Book 6 detail the types of damages that can be claimed under Dutch law. The modified text limits this by introducing 'reasonably foreseeable' and replacing 'death or personal injury' with 'severe discomfort', which is not a defined or legally recognized category of damage, potentially reducing the injured party's legal recourse compared to what is allowed in the code.

Geldend van 01-01-1992 t/m heden Toon relaties in LiDO Maak een permanente link Toon wetstechnische informatie Geen andere versie om mee te vergelijken Druk de regeling af Sla de regeling op Origineel opschrift en aanhef Wet van 9 oktober 1991, tot wijziging van de Wet uitkeringen vervolgingsslachtoffers 1940-1945 en de Wet uitkeringen burger-oorlogsslachtoffers 1940-1945 (invoering gelijke behandeling van mannen en vrouwen alsmede enige andere wijzigingen) Wij Beatrix, bij de gratie Gods, Koningin der Nederlanden, Prinses van Oranje-Nassau, enz. enz. enz. Allen, die deze zullen zien of horen lezen, saluut! doen te weten: Alzo Wij in overweging genomen hebben, dat het wenselijk is gelijke behandeling van mannen en vrouwen in te voeren in de Wet uitkeringen vervolgingsslachtoffers 1940-1945 ( Stb. 1986, 386) en de Wet uitkeringen burger-oorlogsslachtoffers 1940-1945 ( Stb. 1984, 94), alsmede enige andere wijzigingen in die wetten aan te brengen; Zo is het, dat Wij, de Raad van State gehoord, en met gemeen overleg der Staten-Generaal, hebben goedgevonden en verstaan, gelijk Wij goedvinden en verstaan bij deze: Artikel I Toon relaties in LiDO Maak een permanente link Toon wetstechnische informatie Geen andere versie om mee te vergelijken Druk het regelingonderdeel af Sla het regelingonderdeel op [Red: Bevat wijzigingen in andere regelgeving.] Artikel II Toon relaties in LiDO Maak een permanente link Toon wetstechnische informatie Geen andere versie om mee te vergelijken Druk het regelingonderdeel af Sla het regelingonderdeel op [Red: Bevat wijzigingen in andere regelgeving.] Artikel III Toon relaties in LiDO Maak een permanente link Toon wetstechnische informatie Geen andere versie om mee te vergelijken Druk het regelingonderdeel af Sla het regelingonderdeel op - 1. Bij de inwerkingtreding van deze wet reeds toegekende rechten ingevolge de Wet uitkeringen vervolgingsslachtoffers 1940-1945 of de Wet uitkeringen burger-oorlogsslachtoffers 1940-1945 worden bij de inwerkingtreding van deze wet niet opnieuw getoetst aan de artikelen van deze wetten zoals deze dan komen te luiden behoudens voor zover het betreft de artikelen 17 en 19 van de Wet uitkeringen vervolgingsslachtoffers 1940-1945 en 23 en 28 van de Wet uitkeringen burger-oorlogsslachtoffers 1940-1945 . Indien ingevolge de vorige volzin de artikelen 17 en 19 van de Wet uitkeringen vervolgingsslachtoffers 1940-1945 , onderscheidenlijk de artikelen 23 en 28 van de Wet uitkeringen burger-oorlogsslachtoffers 1940-1945 worden toegepast op een ongehuwde persoon die bij de inwerkingtreding van deze wet duurzaam samenwoonde in de zin van artikel 10, vijfde en zesde lid, van de Wet uitkeringen verv
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces terms ('reasonably foreseeable damage', 'severe discomfort') that deviate from established legal categories for damages, potentially reducing legal recourse. The original text mentions 'direct damage to physical goods (property damage or zaakschade) or directly resulting from death or personal injury', which aligns with established legal principles. The Perturbation Explanation states that 'Severe discomfort' is not a legally recognized category, and 'reasonably foreseeable' adds a subjective element to property damage, contradicting established liability principles.

perturbation.original_text: 14.1 Non-performance by either Party of its obligations pursuant to this Agreement or delay in performing the same shall not constitute a breach of the Agreement if and for as long as it is due to a force majeure event, including, but not limited to, government action or requirement of regulatory authority, lock-outs, strikes, shortage of transportation, war, terrorist attacks, rebellion or other military action, fire, floods, natural catastrophes, that a Party is not able to overcome with reasonable and proportional efforts, or a non-performance of obligations by a sub-contractor to a Party - in circumstances where it is reasonably impossible for that Party or an other sub-contractor to perform such obligation instead -, pursuant to any of the aforementioned reasons.
perturbation.changed_text: 14.1 Non-performance by either Party of its obligations pursuant to this Agreement or delay in performing the same shall not constitute a breach of the Agreement if and for as long as it is due to a force majeure event, including, but not limited to, government suggestion, minor labor disputes, slight delays in transportation, that a Party is not able to overcome with preferred methods.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of Force Majeure has been altered, reducing its scope to include 'government suggestion', 'minor labor disputes' and 'slight delays in transportation', and changing the standard of effort needed to overcome it to 'preferred methods'. This weakens the concept of Force Majeure because legally, a force majeure event needs to be something truly exceptional. Under Dutch law and general legal principles, 'force majeure' requires an unforeseeable and insurmountable obstacle. This modification lowers the threshold for what constitutes a force majeure event, potentially conflicting with its legal definition.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Burgerlijk Wetboek (Civil Code) - specifically articles related to force majeure (overmacht), typically found in Book 6 concerning obligations and contracts.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 75 of Book 6 of the Dutch Civil Code addresses circumstances of 'overmacht' (force majeure). The changed text contradicts this law because it includes circumstances like 'government suggestion,' 'minor labor disputes,' and 'slight delays in transportation' under force majeure. Article 75 requires circumstances that are unforeseeable and unavoidable despite reasonable measures. The modified terms do not meet this high threshold, potentially allowing a party to avoid contractual obligations for trivial reasons, contrary to the intent and application of force majeure under Dutch law.

perturbation.justification: The changed text broadens the definition of force majeure to include events like 'government suggestion', 'minor labor disputes', and 'slight delays in transportation,' while also weakening the required effort to overcome such events to 'preferred methods.' This contradicts the legal principle of force majeure, which, as explained in the LLM explanation, requires unforeseeable and unavoidable circumstances despite reasonable measures. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing Artikel 75 within Afdeling 9 De gevolgen van het niet nakomen van een verbintenis, pertains to circumstances of force majeure. The modified terms do not meet this high threshold, as force majeure events must be insurmountable and unforeseeable obstacles, making the contract unenforceable.
